coredata.dc:title: Rationally designed tetra (4-carboxyphenyl) porphyrin/graphene quantum dots/bismuth molybdate Z-scheme heterojunction for tetracycline degradation and Cr(VI) reduction: Performance, mechanism, intermediate toxicity appraisement
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

Li, Shijie
Cai, Mingjie
Yan, Ruyu
Dong, Kexin
Zhang, Junlei
Liu, Yanping
coredata.dc:description: Constructing novel Z-scheme heterojunctions is an effective strategy for obtaining high-performance photocatalysts. Herein, tetra (4-carboxyphenyl) porphyrin (TCPP) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were loaded on the surface of Bi2MoO6 (BMO) to fabricate novel Z-scheme heterojunctions of TCPP/G/BMO. Especially, TCPP/G/BMO-2 showed an exceptional visible-light photoactivity for tetracycline (TC) degradation (81.0%, 40 min) and Cr(VI) reduction (90.7%, 60 min), respectively by 2.38 folds and 2.96 folds enhancement compared to sole Bi2MoO6. The substantial enhancement of activity is attributed to the synergy effect of the Z-scheme charge transfer mechanism and the improved light absorption. The degradation pathways of TC were inferred by determining the generated intermediates using high performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS), and the toxicity of the transformation products was assessed by Toxicity Estimation Software Tool (T.E.S.T). Overall, on the basis of trapping experiments and electron spin resonance spectra (ESR) analysis, the photocatalytic mechanisms of Cr(VI) reduction and TC degradation by the TCPP/G/BMO Z-scheme heterojunction was proposed. This work indicates TCPP/G/BMO could be a promising photocatalyst for wastewater treatment.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The well designed TCPP/GQDs/Bi2MoO6 Z-scheme heterojunction can be utilized as a highly efficient and stable photocatalyst for pharmaceutical antibiotic degradation and Cr(VI) reduction under visible light.

Constructing novel Z-scheme heterojunctions is an effective strategy for obtaining high-performance photocatalysts. Herein, tetra (4-carboxyphenyl) porphyrin (TCPP) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were loaded on the surface of Bi2MoO6 (BMO) to fabricate novel Z-scheme heterojunctions of TCPP/G/BMO. Especially, TCPP/G/BMO-2 showed an exceptional visible-light photoactivity for tetracycline (TC) degradation (81.0%, 40 min) and Cr(VI) reduction (90.7%, 60 min), respectively by 2.38 folds and 2.96 folds enhancement compared to sole Bi2MoO6. The substantial enhancement of activity is attributed to the synergy effect of the Z-scheme charge transfer mechanism and the improved light absorption. The degradation pathways of TC were inferred by determining the generated intermediates using high performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS), and the toxicity of the transformation products was assessed by Toxicity Estimation Software Tool (T.E.S.T). Overall, on the basis of trapping experiments and electron spin resonance spectra (ESR) analysis, the photocatalytic mechanisms of Cr(VI) reduction and TC degradation by the TCPP/G/BMO Z-scheme heterojunction was proposed. This work indicates TCPP/G/BMO could be a promising photocatalyst for wastewater treatment.

ce:section.ce:para: Eliminating harmful pollutants (e.g., antibiotics and heavy metal ions) in aqueous environment has stimulated tremendous interest globally due to the great threat of the worsening environment to human health [1,2]. Semiconductor photocatalysis that merely utilizes photocatalysts and natural sunlight has been extremely appealing for environmental protection as a promising “green” technology [3–7]. The exploration of novel photocatalysts with excellent utilization capacity of sunlight and charge separation rate has been of utmost significance for the practical application [8–21].

Bismuth molybdate (Bi2MoO6) as one of the most promising visible–light–driven Bi–based photocatalysts has been extensively studied in the field of photocatalysis because of its chemical stability, cost-effectiveness, environmental friendliness and narrow bandgap. Despite these merits, tremendous challenges are still in the way toward actual applications of Bi2MoO6 such as low sunlight response and severe recombination of electron-hole pairs, and so forth, resulting in unsatisfactory photocatalytic potency [22]. To conquer such drawbacks, various strategies have been developed, including noble metal deposition [23], elemental doping [24], defect engineering [25,26], and heterojunction fabrication [27–31]. Among them, the fabrication of novel heterojunctions is deemed as an effective route to upgrade the photocatalytic performance [27–29].

Very currently, the artificial Z-scheme heterojunctions have aroused tremendous attention by virtue of their efficient spatial disassociation of photo-generated charge carriers with the optimum redox capability for catalytic reactions [32]. Correspondingly, the design of Z-scheme heterojunction is a viable strategy for enhancing the catalytic performance [33–44]. Notably, the catalytic potency of the direct Z-scheme heterojunction can be further improved through the introduction of an electron shuttle mediator. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) has been demonstrated to be one of the best electron shuttle mediators because of excellent electric conductivity and sunlight harvesting capability [45–50]. Hence, the development of a Z-scheme heterojunction of combining Bi2MoO6, GQDs and other proper semiconductor is highly anticipated.

Porphyrin and its derivatives, as an important type of organic semiconductors, have been attracting increasing interest because of the extraordinary broad-spectrum response, and adjustable band structure [51–54]. Of note, mesotetra (4-carboxyphenyl) porphyrin (TCPP) as a porphyrin-based supramolecular material has been applied in the photocatalytic field, while the high recombination probability of charge carriers is also hindering its photocatalytic activity [55,56]. More importantly, the band energy structure of TCPP matches with that of Bi2MoO6, making for the construction of the TCPP/Bi2MoO6 Z-scheme heterojunction.

Considering the matched band structure of Bi2MoO6 and TCPP, as well as the advantages of the GQDs, it is worthy to expect for designing a novel Z-scheme heterojunction of TCPP/G/BMO for efficient removal of TC and Cr(VI). Hence, a facile solvothermal method was developed to fabricate the TCPP/G/BMO heterojunction, which manifested a prominently enhanced photocatalytic behavior compared to the pristine BMO, TCPP and the binary heterojunctions. The degradation routes, toxicity evolution of TC as well as the improved photocatalytic mechanism were elucidated.

Bismuth nitrate pentahydrate (Bi(NO3)3·5H2O, ≥99.0%), sodium molybdate dihydrate (Na2MoO4·2H2O, ≥99.0%), tetracycline hydrochloride (TC, 98.0%), sodium chloride (NaCl, ≥99.8%), sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO3, ≥99.5%), sodium nitrate (NaNO3, ≥99.0%), sodium sulfate (Na2SO4, ≥99.0%), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, ≥96.0%), potassium dichromate (K2Cr2O7, ≥99.8%), silver nitrate (AgNO3, ≥99.8%), ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt (EDTA-2Na, ≥99.0%), isoPropyl alcohol (IPA, ≥99.0%), hydrochloric acid (HCl, 36.0～38.0%), ethylene glycol (EG, ≥99.0%), and ethanol absolute (≥99.7%) were provided by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Meso-tetra (4-carboxyphenyl) porphyrin (TCPP, 97.0%), nitrotetrazolium blue chloride (NBT, 98%), humic acid (HA, ≥90%) were provided by Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. 4-Hydroxy-TEMPO (98%) and triethanolamine (TEOA, ≥99.0%) were provided by Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Graphene quantum dots (GQDs, 99%) were provided by Nanjing XFNANO Materials Tech Co., Ltd. All chemicals were used directly and without any further purification, and all aqueous solutions were prepared with the deionized water (18.0 MΩ cm).

Bi(NO3)3·5H2O (0.4850 g, 1 mmol) and Na2MoO4·2H2O (0.1210 g, 0.5 mmol) were sequentially introduced into the solution of EG (20 mL) + ethanol (18 mL) under constant stirring for 30 min. After the chemicals were completely dissolved, the formed transparent solution was transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and maintained at 160 °C for 20 h to yield a pale-yellow product. The product was rinsed by ultra-pure water and ethanol for four times and then was dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C for 24 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of TCPP/GQDs/Bi2MoO6 (denoted as TCPP/G/BMO) heterojunctions
ce:section.ce:para: TCPP/G/BMO heterojunctions were synthesized by a facile solvothermal method (Fig. 1 ). 1 mmol of Bi(NO3)3·5H2O and 0.5 mmol of Na2MoO4·5H2O were sequentially dissolved in the solution of EG (20 mL) + ethanol (18 mL) under constant agitation, followed by the addition of 3 mg of GQDs and a certain dose of TCPP. The suspension was placed in an ultrasonic bath for 1 h to obtain a homogeneous suspension. Subsequently, the suspension after being stirred for another 3 h was subjected to solvothermal reaction at 160 °C for 20 h. Next, the product was washed thoroughly and dried at 60 °C. The TCPP/G/BMO samples with varying contents of TCPP (0.05, 0.1, and 0.5 wt%) were defined as TCPP/G/BMO-1, TCPP/G/BMO-2 and TCPP/G/BMO-3, respectively. For comparison, G/BMO (TCPP/BMO) was synthesized in the absence of TCPP (GQDs) under the same conditions.
The detailed characterization methods are shown in Supplementary Material.

The photocatalytic experiments for TC degradation and Cr(VI) reduction were carried out under visible light using a 300 W xenon lamp adapted with a UV cutoff filter (λ > 420 nm) [57,58]. Typically, 10 mg or 20 mg of photocatalyst was first dispersed in of TC solution (80 mL, 20 mg/L) or Cr(VI) solution (80 mL, 10 mg/L) in a 200 mL glass reactor. The temperature was maintained at room temperature using a water-cooling system and the distance between light and the pollutant solution is 15 cm. Prior to reactions, the suspension was agitated for 30 min in darkness to guarantee the adsorption–desorption equilibrium. The photocatalytic reaction was monitored by sampling the solution (1 mL) at certain intervals and analyzed utilizing UV–vis spectroscopy with the characteristic absorbance peak of 356 nm for TC. The concentration of Cr(VI) was analyzed utilizing UV–vis spectroscopy (540 nm) according to the 1,5-diphenylcarbazide method [59]. In the cycle experiment, the used catalysts were recycled after each photoreaction, washed four times by deionized water, and then dried at 70 °C for next run.

The mineralization efficiency of TC solutions during reaction was calculated by (TOC0-TOCt)/TOC0 × 100% (1), where TOC0 and TOCt represent the initial and time t TOC values, respectively. Each photocatalytic test was implemented in triplicate, and error bars represent the standard deviations calculated from three independent experimental results.

The radical trapping experiments were implemented employing isopropanol (IPA), nitro-tetrazolium blue chloride (NBT), EDTA-2Na and AgNO3 as quenchers of •OH, •O2 –, h+ and e–, respectively [35]. Bruker 300E ESR spectrometer was applied to identify •O2 – and •OH with 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) as a trapping agent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Degradation intermediates detection and toxicity analyses
ce:section.ce:para: The detailed methods for degradation intermediates detection and toxicity analyses are shown in Supplementary Material

The crystal structures of TCPP, BMO, TCPP/BMO, G/BMO, and TCPP/G/BMO heterojunctions were investigated by X-ray diffraction (XRD) (Fig. 2
a). The XRD pattern of BMO shows the diffraction peaks at 2θ = 28.2°, 32.5°, 47.1°, 55.5° and 58.4° corresponding to the (131), (200), (260), (133) and (262) planes of the orthorhombic BMO (JCPDS NO. 76–2388) [60–62]. The XRD pattern of TCPP shows a weak and broad diffraction peak at 2θ = 10–30°, in line with the previously reported phenomenon [57]. For the G/BMO, TCPP/BMO and TCPP/G/BMO heterojunctions, only diffraction peaks of BMO are detected with no characteristic peaks of TCPP and GQDs due to relatively low amount of TCPP and GQDs. Moreover, no impurity phases are detected, revealing the high purity of the synthesized samples.

The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) analysis was conducted to inspect the chemical bonding interactions of TCPP/G/BMO. From Fig. 2 b, the C–OH skeletal vibration (1257.6 cm−1), –COO– structural vibration (1404.2 cm−1), CC stretching vibration (1600.0 cm−1) and CO vibrations (1697.4 cm−1) are detected in the spectrum of TCPP [63]. Peaks situated at 1380, 1624, and 1061 cm−1 correspond to the C–OH bending, C C stretching, and CO stretching in GQDs [64]. For BMO, the broad bands at 563.2 cm−1, 732.9 cm−1 and the sharp bands at 841.0 cm−1 are assigned to the stretching vibrations of Bi–O, Mo–O and bridging stretching vibrations of Mo–O–Mo, respectively [65]. For TCPP/G/BMO-2, the characteristic peaks of TCPP, GQDs and BMO are observed, signifying the successful preparation of the TCPP/G/BMO heterojunction.

The surface chemical information of Bi2MoO6 and TCPP/G/BMO-2 was characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). From Fig. 3
a, it can be seen that all the elements (Bi, Mo, C, N, and O) of TCPP/G/BMO-2 are detected in the XPS survey spectrum of TCPP/G/BMO-2. For pristine BMO, the characteristic peaks at 158.6 eV and 163.9 eV in Fig. 3 b could be ascribed to Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2 of Bi3+, respectively [24,58,66]. From Fig. 3 c, the two peaks at 231.8 eV and 235.0 eV in the spectrum of BMO can be assigned to Mo 3d5/2 and Mo 3d3/2 of Mo6+, respectively [67,68]. Besides, the O 1 s peak in Fig. 3 f for BMO can be de-convoluted into two peaks at 529.4 eV and 531.0 eV, attributed to Bi–O and Mo–O bonds, respectively [26]. Compared with pure BMO, the Bi 4f, Mo 3d, and O 1 s binding energies of TCPP/G/BMO-2 all present a negative shift of ∼ 0.1–0.3 eV, which demonstrates the strong chemical interactions between BMO and TCPP/GQDs. Additionally, the O 1 s peak at 532.4 eV of TCPP/G/BMO-2 may correspond to the CO from GQDs or O H from TCPP [64,69]. As displayed in Fig. 3 d, four peaks of C 1 s centered at 284.3, 285.2, 285.8 and 288.0 eV correspond to the C C, C O, CO and OC − O bonds, respectively [64]. From Fig. 3 e, the N 1 s spectrum of TCPP/G/BMO-2 shows three peaks located at 397.8, 399.4, and 400.3 eV, corresponding to the CN − C, N H and N C bonds of the N1s, respectively [70]. Combining the XPS data with FT-IR data suggests the successful preparation of the TCPP/G/BMO-2 heterojunction.

Fig. 4 presents the scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of BMO and TCPP/G/BMO-2, the EDS elemental mapping images of TCPP/G/BMO-2. Distinctly, bare BMO displays a hierarchical flower-like architecture (diameter: 1–1.5 μm) assembled by numerous 2D nanosheets and its surface is smooth. After the introduction of GQDs and TCPP, the obtained TCPP/G/BMO-2 retains the hierarchical structure with rough surfaces due to the load of TCPP and GQDs. Further, the EDS spectrum ( Figure S1 ) and the elemental mapping images (Fig. 4 e) were collected to explore the surface element composition of TCPP/G/BMO-2. Clearly, the EDS spectrum and elemental mapping images evidence the coexistence and good distribution of Bi, Mo, O, C and N elements on the surface of TCPP/G/BMO-2. As presented in Figure S2
a, b, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM images of TCPP/G/BMO-2 were collected to further analyze the microstructure, which exhibits a hierarchical microsphere with TCPP and GQDs covered on the surface, confirming the formation of the TCPP/G/BMO heterojunction with intimately interfacial contacts. Furthermore, the detected lattice fringe with d values of 0.32 nm is can be ascribed to the (002) facet from BMO ( Figure S2
b).

Tetracycline (TC), a typical clinical antibiotic that has been frequently detected in various aqueous environmental media, was selected as the model to assess the photoactivity of the as-prepared catalysts. From Fig. 6 a, the blank test manifests that the TC is stable without catalysts under visible light irradiation. Only 41.5% and 0.8% of TC is degraded within 40 min by bare BMO and TCPP, revealing their poor photocatalytic potency. The binary heterojunctions show markedly upgraded photoactivity (53.6% for TCPP/BMO, 45.8% for G/BMO), which is ascribed to the better light absorption and charge separation because of the formation of heterojunctions. More inspiringly, the ternary Z-scheme heterojunctions of TCPP/G/BMO manifest prominently superior TC degradation performance as compared to the pristine ones and the binary heterojunctions, due to the synergistic effect of TCPP, GQD and BMO. In particular, TCPP/G/BMO-2 composite acquires the most potent photoactivity with a TC degradation efficiency of up to 81.0%. Of note, loading excessive TCPP weakens the catalytic performance of TCPP/G/BMO. Furthermore, the photo-degradation kinetic plots of all catalysts were acquired by the pseudo-first-order model: –Ln (Ct/C0) = kt (2), where k represents the rate constant, Ct and C0 mean the Cr(VI) concentration at t and 0, respectively. From Fig. 5
b, the calculated k follows the sequence TCPP/G/BMO-2 > TCPP/G/BMO-1 > TCPP/G/BMO-3 > TCPP/BMO > Mixture > G/BMO > BMO > TCPP. Clearly, TCPP/G/BMO-2 obtains the largest k value, manifesting a 2.38 and 1.02 times enhancement than that of BMO and TCPP/BMO, respectively. Note that the photoactivity of TCPP/G/BMO-2 outperforms or is comparable to a wealth of Bi2MoO6-based ternary heterojunction photocatalysts (Table 1 ).

The effect of pH on TC degradation over TCPP/G/BMO-2 was explored in broad pH window (4, 5.5, 7, 8.5 and 10) (Fig. 5 c). Clearly, pH exerts a significant impact on the TC degradation by TCPP/G/BMO-2. Specifically, TCPP/G/BMO-2 exhibits the maximum activity (81.0%) at pH = 5.5. The strong acid environment (pH = 4) or alkaline environment (pH = 8.5 or 10) is not conducive to the degradation of TC, which is probably originated from the surface charge evolution of the photocatalyst and TC with pH.

Various anions including Cl–, HCO3 –, NO3 – and SO4 2– are commonly present in natural bodies, which pose a potential impact on the photoactivity of a catalyst. Thus, the effect of these coexisting anions on TC removal by TCPP/G/BMO-2 was investigated. From Fig. 5 d, these anions present a hindering impact on TC degradation in varying degrees, and the TC degradation efficiency is decreased from 81.0% to 76.4%, 61.4%, 78.0%, and 42.6% in the presence of Cl–, HCO3 –, NO3 –, and SO4 2–, respectively. Distinctly, Cl– and NO3 – exert minor effect on the catalytic process. By contrast, SO4 2– or HCO3 – exhibits the markedly inhibitory effect, which is due to that SO4 2– or HCO3 – could react with hydroxyl radicals (•OH) to generate less-reactive species [74], undermining the photoactivity of TCPP/G/BMO-2.

Humic acid (HA) is a ubiquitous organic matter in natural aqueous environment, and its concentration of HA in the river or groundwater is around 10 mg/L. Thus, HA with a concentration in the range of 0–10 mg/L was chose to examine its influence on TC removal by TCPP/G/BMO-2. From Fig. 5 e, with HA concentration increasing from 0 to 10 mg/L, TC degradation efficiency decreases moderately from 81.0% to 75.0%. This inhibitory effect is due to that HA could compete with the TC for the active substances, especially ·OH radicals [75]. Meanwhile, HA could function as light filter, quenching the production of active species during photocatalysis [76].

The mineralization degree of organic matters is another significant parameter to appraise the photocatalytic properties of the catalyst. The mineralization capability of TCPP/G/BMO-2 was inspected by TOC measurements. From Fig. 5 f, the TOC removal efficiency is up to about 33.1% for TCPP/G/BMO-2 after 60 min of visible light irradiation. This fact evidences that TCPP/G/BMO-2 possesses a favorable mineralization capability, holding a vast potential for wastewater treatment.

The stability of TCPP/G/BMO-2 was explored through a repeated usage for the degradation of TC. No apparent loss of the photocatalytic potency of TCPP/G/BMO-2 has been detected for at least five runs (Fig. 6 a), and the XRD characterizations (Fig. 6 b) reflect that no structural variations occurs after the cycling test, evidencing the favorable reusability and stability of TCPP/G/BMO-2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: TC degradation pathways and toxicity assessment
ce:section.ce:para:
The Maldi-TOF-MS was applied to analyze the degradation products of TC by TCPP/G/BMO-2 ( Figure S3
a, b and Table S1), and two possible degradation pathways of TC were brought up (Fig. 7
a). In pathway I, P1 (m/z = 458) is formed through the hydroxylation of enol structure of TC under the attack of •OH radicals [57]. The generation of P2 (m/z = 447) and P3 (m/z = 433) is realized by successive demethylation processes. Subsequently, the amino group of P3 is oxidized into = O group, which further undergoes the hydroxylation reaction due to the attack of •OH, leading to the generation of P4 (m/z = 449) radicals [77]. P4 experiences the ring-opening reaction, producing P5 (m/z = 496). P5 further undergoes an elimination reaction to obtain P6 (m/z = 441). In pathway II, The CN bond on the amide group is relatively weak and readily attacked by •OH radicals, thus the TC molecule experiences N-oxidation to form the P7 (m/z = 446) [7], which could be converted to P8 (m/z = 399) and P9 (m/z = 419) by decarboxylation and hydroxylation reaction. As the reaction goes on, P9 is degraded into smaller molecules (e.g., P10 (m/z = 214), P11 (m/z = 158) and P12 (m/z = 141) through a series of oxidation, ring opening and elimination processes. Eventually, these compounds will be cleaved and mineralized into CO2, H2O and other smaller substances.

Toxicity Estimation Software (T.E.S.T.) based on quantitative structure–activity relationship (QSAR) prediction was utilized to appraise the toxicity (e.g., acute toxicity, developmental toxicity and mutagenicity) of TC and its degradation products (Fig. 7 b-dand Table S2). From Fig. 7 b, (i) TC with a low LC50 is categorized as “toxic”; (ii) all the intermediates (except P1, P7, and P8) have greater LC50 than that of TC. In particular, P5, P6, P10, P11 and P12 are even “not harmful”. From Fig. 7 c, the developmental toxicity of all the intermediates is much lower than that of TC, signifying that the catalytic reaction can effectively alleviate the developmental toxicity of TC. From Fig. 7 d, (i) TC is mutagenicity positive; (ii) all the intermediates (except P1, P4 and P8) show a decrease in the mutagenicity; (iii) P5, P6, P9, P10, P11, and P12 are mutagenicity negative. These facts verify that the acute toxicity, developmental toxicity, and mutagenicity of TC can be effectively abated by the TCPP/G/BMO-2 photocatalytic system.

The photocatalytic behavior of these as-synthesized samples for the photo-reduction of Cr(VI) was also tested under visible light irradiation (Fig. 8
a, b). The control experiment illustrates the self-reduction Cr(VI) is negligible without catalysts within 60 min of light irradiation. Pure BMO and TCPP perform low Cr(VI) reduction efficiency of 49.6% and 8.6%, respectively. After building heterojunctions, the obtained G/BMO, TCPP/BMO, TCPP/G/BMO-1, TCPP/G/BMO-2 and TCPP/G/BMO-3 samples display enhanced photocatalytic potency, accomplishing 64.0%, 71.9%, 84.5%, 90.7% and 79.5% of Cr(VI) reduction within 60 min, which is far superior to BMO and TCPP. Especially, TCPP/G/BMO-3 shows the highest Cr(VI) reduction behavior, indicating that the introduction of GQDs and TCPP into TCPP/G/BMO-2 not only strengthens the sunlight harvesting, but also propels the disassociation of charge carriers. Furthermore, TCPP/G/BMO-2 shows a much higher Cr(VI) reduction efficiency than the mixture prepared by physically mixing, evidencing the pivotal role of intense interfacial contact in boosting the photocatalytic potency. Additionally, the kinetics of photo-reduction process of Cr(VI) were revealed by the pseudo-first-order model. From Fig. 8 b, the k values of the BMO, TCPP, G/BMO, TCPP/BMO, TCPP/G/BMO-1, TCPP/G/BMO-2, TCPP/G/BMO-3, and mixture are 0.0092 min−1, 0.0163 min−1, 0.0225 min−1, 0.0302 min−1, 0.0364 min−1, 0.0266 min−1, and 0.0118 min−1, respectively. Intriguingly, TCPP/G/BMO-2 performs the highest k, which is nearly 3.96, 33.1 or 1.6 folds that of BMO, TCPP or TCPP/BMO.

To inspect the impact of pH on Cr(VI) reduction, a series of experiments were implemented under various pH conditions ranging from 2.5 to 10.5 (Fig. 8 c). It is noted that Cr(VI) reduction is improved gradually with decreasing pH, in a good accordance with the reported phenomena [78,79]. The optimal photo-reduction performance is achieved at pH = 2.5. Under acidic condition, more H+ ions are available for the effective reduction of Cr(VI) to Cr(III) (Eqs. (3) and (4) ). By contrast, under basic condition, the formed reduction product (Cr(OH)3) of CrO4 2– is prone to generate precipitates (Eq. (5) ) that deposit on TCPP/G/BMO-2, hampering the photo-reduction of Cr(VI).
(3) Cr2O7 2– + 14H+ + 6 e– → 2Cr3+ + 7H2O
(4) HCrO4 – + 7H+ + 3e– → Cr3+ + 4H2O
(5) CrO4 2– + 4H2O + 3e– → Cr(OH)3 + 5OH–

In terms of the actual application, the stability of TCPP/G/BMO-2 for Cr(VI) reduction was evaluated. From Fig. 8 d, TCPP/G/BMO-2 still achieves a 81.9% Cr(VI) reduction efficiency in the fifth run, further verifying the prominent stability.

The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) specific surface area, as a crucial factor that influences the adsorption-photocatalysis behavior, was also explored [80,81]. As displayed in Fig. 9 , BMO and TCPP/G/BMO-2 possess the type-IV isotherms based on the IUPAC classification, which indicate the mesoporous structure characteristic. TCPP/G/BMO-2 exhibits a much smaller BET surface area (51.58 m2/g) than pristine BMO (72.51 m2/g), signifying that the BET surface area is not the critical factor in improving the catalytic performance of TCPP/G/BMO-2.

The optical properties of TCPP, BMO, TCPP/BMO, G/BMO and TCPP/G/BMO heterojunctions were investigated by UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (UV–vis DRS) (Fig. 10
a). Compared with BMO, G/BMO shows enhanced light absorption in the UV–Vis region. After the combination with TCPP molecules, the obtained TCPP/G/BMO heterojunctions exhibit better light absorption capacity. Of note, a broad visible-light absorption band (500–750 nm) is detected in the TCPP/G/BMO heterojunctions, which is attributed to the π–π staking interaction and the aggregation of TCPP. Moreover, the band gap energy (Eg) of samples can be calculated by the Kubelka–Munk function: αhv = A (hv - Eg ) n
/2 (6), the Eg of BMO and TCPP are determined to be 2.73 and 1.65 eV, respectively (Fig. 10 b).

In addition, the conduction band (CB) position of BMO was investigated by Mott − Schottky curves (Fig. 10 c). The flat-band potential (Efb) of BMO is estimated to be − 0.44 V vs Ag/AgCl by the Mott–Schottky analysis. The Efb is equivalent to − 0.24 V vs normal hydrogen electrode (NHE) based on ENHE = EAg/AgCl + 0.197 (7). Considering that the Efb of an n-type semiconductor is 0.1 V more positive than its conduction band edge potential (ECB), the ECB of BMO is − 0.34 V (vs. NHE). On basis of the Eg (2.73 eV) of BMO, the VB potential of BMO is calculated to be 2.39 V by using the formula: ECB = EVB – Eg (8).

The photo-induced electron-hole recombination rate generally plays a significant role in the process of photocatalytic reaction [82–87]. The transient photocurrent response (TPR) and electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) Nyquist plots were recorded to inspect the separation and migration behavior of the charge carriers in the photocatalysts. From Fig. 10 d, it is distinct that all the samples exhibit steady and reproducible photocurrent responses upon repetitive visible-light irradiation. The photocurrent intensity of TCPP/G/BMO ternary heterojunctions is larger than that of bare BMO and binary heterojunctions (G/BMO or TCPP/BMO) under visible light, evidencing the improved separation efficiency of photo-induced electron–hole pairs in ternary heterojunctions [88]. Among these ternary heterojunctions, TCPP/G/BMO-2 shows the highest photocurrent intensity, indicating the most efficient separation of photo-generated electron-hole pairs. Additionally, Fig. 10 e shows the EIS Nyquist plots of all the as-fabricated samples. Usually, a smaller EIS arc radius means a minor charge transport resistance of electrode [67,89–91]. Clearly, the impedance arc radius of the samples follows the order: TCPP/G/BMO-2 < TCPP/G/BMO-1 < TCPP/G/BMO-3 < G/BMO < TCPP/BMO < BMO, which is in line with the results of the TPR tests. In particular, TCPP/G/BMO-2 has the smallest arc diameter, reflecting that TCPP/G/BMO-2 possesses the excellent interface electron transfer rate.

The photoluminescence (PL) spectra of BMO and TCPP/G/BMO-2 were recorded for unclosing the electrons/holes separation rate. As is known, the weaker emission peak intensity works in concert with lower recombination of electron-hole pairs [92–97]. It can be observed from Fig. 10 f that TCPP/G/BMO-2 exhibits much weaker PL intensity than BMO, reflecting that the construction of Z-scheme heterojunction appreciably hinders the recombination of charge carriers.
The above results verify that the most efficient spatial electrons/holes separation and transport is achieved in TCPP/G/BMO-2, which mainly accounts for the superior photocatalytic activity.

To elucidate the photocatalytic mechanism, a series of trapping tests were implemented utilizing isopropanol (IPA), nitro-tetrazolium blue chloride (NBT), EDTA-2Na and AgNO3 as quenchers of •OH, •O2 –, h+ and e–, respectively. Form Fig. 11
a, the TC degradation is substantially inhibited by the introduction of NBT, EDTA-2Na and IPA, respectively, evidencing the key role of h+, •O2 – and •OH in TC degradation. From Fig. 11 b, NBT or IPA exhibits a slight quenching influence on Cr(VI) reduction, reflecting the minor role of •O2 – or •OH in Cr(VI) reduction. EDTA-2Na shows a positive effect on the reduction of Cr(VI), indicating that more photo-excited e– is separately and engaged in the photo-reduction of Cr(VI) due to effective capture of photo-excited h+. By contrast, the Cr(VI) reduction is evidently suppressed in the presence of AgNO3, confirming the crucial role of e– in Cr(VI) reduction.

Further, the electron spin resonance spectra (ESR) characterizations were executed to inspect the charge transfer mechanism. From Fig. 11 c, d, the ESR signals (DMPO–•O2 – and •OH) of BMO and TCPP/G/BMO are detected, reflecting that both pristine BMO and TCPP/G/BMO can induce the formation of •O2 – and •OH radicals under visible light. Noteworthily, the ESR signal intensity of TCPP/G/BMO-2 is much stronger than that of bare BMO, evidencing that more •O2 – and •OH radicals generated account for the upgraded photocatalytic potency. To sum up, the above capture tests and ESR characterizations certify that the charge transfer pathway belongs to the Z-scheme mechanism, which favors the spatial disassociation of electrons and holes with better redox capacity in the TCPP/G/BMO-2 heterojunction, therefore boosting the generation of more •O2 – and •OH species.

In view of the above experimental results, the mechanism for the improved photocatalytic performance of the TCPP/G/BMO heterojunction is proposed (Fig. 12 ). The synergy effect of Z-scheme charge transfer pathway and the enhanced sunlight absorption leads to the superior photocatalytic performance of TCPP/G/BMO for TC degradation and Cr(VI) reduction compared with BMO, TCPP, TCPP/BMO and G/BMO. According to Mott-Schottky plot and DRS spectrum, the ECB and EVB of Bi2MoO6 are –0.34 eV and 2.39 eV, respectively. It has been reported that the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of TCPP are 1.04 eV and –0.61 eV, respectively. The hetero-structure with a staggered band alignment is conducive to the separation and transport of electrons and holes at the interface. Of note, the type-II transport behavior of charge carriers commonly leads to the weakened redox potential, which is not beneficial to the generation of active substances. Furthermore, the h+ on the HOMO of TCPP is not capable of producing •OH due to the more negative EVB than the redox potential of OH–/•OH (1.99 eV versus NHE) or H2O/•OH (2.40 eV versus NHE). Nonetheless, the ESR peak (DMPO–•O2 – and •OH) intensity of TCPP/G/BMO is eminently higher than that of BMO, which is contrary with the type-II mechanism. Therefore, the charge migratory behavior should abide by the Z-scheme model. Note that artificial Z-scheme heterojunction can effectively propel the electrons/holes separation and maximize the redox capability of the photocatalytic system [42,98]. When exposed to visible light, e– is activated and transferred from the VB (HOMO) of Bi2MoO6 (TCPP) to CB (LUMO) of Bi2MoO6 (TCPP) (Eq. (9, 10)). The photo-generated e– of BMO rapidly injects into GQDs due to the excellent electrical conductivity, followed by the recombination with the h+ presented in the HOMO of TCPP (Eq. (11)). Meanwhile, the e– with the optimum potent reducing capacity of TCPP is well preserved for the generation of •O2 – (–0.33 eV versus NHE) (Eq. (12)), and the h+ with the most powerful oxidizing capability of BMO VB could react with OH– to yield •OH due to its EVB is more positive than the redox potential of OH–/•OH (1.99 eV versus NHE) (Eq. (13)). The photo-generated e– is primarily engaged in the photo-reduction of Cr(VI) (Eq. (14)), and h+, •O2 – and •OH take part in the decomposition of TC antibiotic (Eq. (15)). Of note, GQDs in the TCPP/G/BMO heterojunction possesses two advantages [47,64]: (i) GQDs serving as charge transferring bridge can accelerate the migration of photo-induced electrons between TCPP and Bi2MoO6, effectively retarding the recombination of charge carriers; (ii) The introduction of GQDs markedly improves the sunlight harvesting capability of the heterojunction. Correspondingly, the TCPP/G/BMO Z-scheme displays an appreciable enhancement of the electron-hole separation efficiency and retains the most potent redox capacity, therefore manifesting the superior photocatalytic performance for the removal of TC and Cr(VI).
(9) BMO + hv → Bi2MoO6 (e− + h+)
(10) TCPP + hv → TCPP (e− + h+)
(11) e− (BMO) + h+ (TCPP) → recombination (GQDs)
(12) e− (TCPP) + O2 → •O2 −
(13) h+ (BMO) + OH− → •OH
(14)
e − + Cr(VI) → Cr (III)
(15) •O2 − + •OH + h+ + TC → CO2 + H2O + Small molecules

In this work, a novel Z-scheme heterojunction of tetra (4-carboxyphenyl) porphyrin /graphene quantum dots/Bi2MoO6 (TCPP/G/BMO) was successfully synthesized by a facile solvothermal method and it demonstrated the excellent photocatalytic elimination of tetracycline (TC) and Cr(VI) (Table 1). The photocatalytic activity of TCPP/G/BMO was substantially upgraded as compared with pure BMO, TCPP, G/BMO, and TCPP/BMO. The boosted catalytic performance is due to the construction of Z-scheme heterojunction, which not only ensures the efficient spatial separation and transfer of charge carriers but also retains the maximum redox capacity for photocatalytic reactions. Active-species trapping tests and ESR characterization reveal that •O2 −, •OH and h+ species dominantly account for the robust photocatalytic decomposition and mineralization of TC, and photo-generated e− mainly participates in the photo-reduction of Cr(VI). This study provides a novel inorganic–organic Z-scheme heterojunction of TCPP/G/BMO with great potential for environmental governance. Further work will focus on the development of easily recyclable and highly efficient inorganic–organic Z-scheme heterojunctions for wastewater treatment.
